questions.questionText: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance between C=O and C-N. This has which structural consequence?
questions.optionA: Free rotation around the peptide bond
questions.optionB: The bond is easily hydrolyzed
questions.optionC: PLANARITY of the peptide bond unit (6 atoms: CÎ±-C(=O)-N(-H)-CÎ± are coplanar), restricting rotation and limiting backbone conformations â€” this is why Ramachandran plots show only certain Ï†/Ïˆ angle combinations are allowed
questions.optionD: All amino acids adopt the same conformation
questions.explanation: Peptide bond â€” structural implications: Resonance: electrons delocalized between C=O and C-N â†’ partial double-bond character for C-N â†’ PLANAR (trans configuration preferred, except Pro which can be cis ~6%). Ramachandran plot: plots backbone dihedral angles Ï† (phi, around N-CÎ±) and Ïˆ (psi, around CÎ±-C). Only ~30% of Ï†/Ïˆ combinations are sterically ALLOWED (no atomic clashes). Î±-helix: Ï† = -57Â°, Ïˆ = -47Â°. Î²-sheet: Ï† = -120Â°, Ïˆ = +120Â° (anti-parallel). Left-handed helix: only glycine (no side chain â†’ no steric clash). Proline: Ï† locked at ~-60Â° (cyclic side chain), further restricts conformational freedom â†’ helix breaker. Peptide bond hydrolysis: thermodynamically favorable but kinetically SLOW (tÂ½ ~500 years at pH 7, 25Â°C) â†’ requires proteases. Reference: Harper's â€” Peptide Bond.

questions.questionText: A patient with osteogenesis imperfecta has a glycine â†’ serine substitution in the Î±1 chain of type I collagen. This is devastating because:
questions.optionB: Serine is hydrophobic
questions.optionC: Glycine (the smallest AA, R=H) must occupy EVERY THIRD position in the collagen triple helix (Gly-X-Y repeat) because only H fits in the crowded interior; ANY substitution at Gly position disrupts triple helix formation causing a dominant-negative effect
questions.optionD: The mutation is always lethal
questions.explanation: Collagen Gly-X-Y â€” structural requirement: Collagen triple helix: 3 left-handed polyproline II helices wound into right-handed superhelix. INTERIOR is extremely TIGHT â€” only glycine (R=H) can fit at every third position. Any larger side chain â†’ steric clash â†’ triple helix disruption. Common OI mutations: Gly â†’ Ser, Gly â†’ Cys, Gly â†’ Arg. DOMINANT-NEGATIVE: one mutant chain disrupts entire triple helix (3 chains involved) â†’ worse than null mutation. 'Suicide model': mutant chains incorporate â†’ disrupt folding from C-terminus to N-terminus â†’ partially unfolded triple helix â†’ susceptible to proteolysis. Severity gradient: mutations closer to C-terminus are MORE severe (folding proceeds Câ†’N). OI types: I (mild, quantitative â†“), II (lethal), III (progressive deforming), IV (moderate). Reference: Harper's â€” OI Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: OI glycine substitution triple helix disruption

questions.questionText: Amino acids are classified as essential or non-essential. The concept of 'conditionally essential' amino acids is clinically relevant because:
questions.optionA: They are never needed in diet
questions.optionB: Tyrosine (from Phe), cysteine (from Met), arginine, glutamine, and proline become essential when their precursor supply is limited or demand exceeds synthesis capacity (e.g., premature neonates, critical illness, inborn errors)
questions.optionC: They are essential only in plants
questions.optionD: All amino acids are equally essential
questions.explanation: Conditionally essential amino acids: Normally NON-ESSENTIAL but become essential under specific conditions: (1) TYROSINE: synthesized from phenylalanine (PAH). In PKU: Tyr becomes essential (no PAH â†’ can't convert Pheâ†’Tyr). In premature infants: immature PAH. (2) CYSTEINE: from methionine via transsulfuration (CBS, cystathionase). In homocystinuria or premature infants: becomes essential. (3) ARGININE: synthesized via urea cycle, but in neonates (especially premature): urea cycle enzymes immature â†’ insufficient synthesis. Critical in NEC (necrotizing enterocolitis) prevention. (4) GLUTAMINE: most abundant AA, fuel for enterocytes/immune cells. In critical illness/burns: demand exceeds synthesis â†’ conditionally essential (ICU supplementation). (5) PROLINE: from glutamate. In wound healing states: demand increases. Essential AAs mnemonic: PVT TIM HaLL. Reference: Harper's â€” Conditionally Essential AA.

questions.questionText: Collagen is unique because it contains:
questions.optionB: Hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine (requires vitamin C for hydroxylation)
questions.optionC: High cysteine content
questions.optionD: Iron-containing heme groups
questions.explanation: Collagen: most abundant protein in the body. Unique features: rich in glycine (every 3rd residue â€” Gly-X-Y repeat), proline and hydroxyproline. Hydroxylation requires vitamin C (ascorbic acid) as cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase. Vitamin C deficiency â†’ scurvy (defective collagen â†’ bleeding gums, poor wound healing, perifollicular hemorrhages). Triple helix structure (3 left-handed chains form right-handed superhelix). Type I (bone, skin) most abundant. Reference: Harper's â€” Collagen.

questions.questionText: Glycine is the only achiral amino acid (no D/L forms). Besides being the simplest AA, glycine has unique metabolic roles:
questions.optionA: Glycine is required for: heme synthesis (with succinyl-CoA), glutathione (Glu-Cys-Gly), purine ring (C4, C5, N7), creatine synthesis, conjugation reactions (bile acids, drugs), collagen (every 3rd residue), and inhibitory neurotransmission (glycinergic synapses in spinal cord)
questions.optionB: It only forms peptide bonds
questions.optionC: It is only used in collagen
questions.optionD: It has no metabolic significance
questions.explanation: Glycine â€” metabolic versatility: Despite being simplest, glycine participates in more biosynthetic reactions than any other AA: (1) HEME: glycine + succinyl-CoA â†’ ALA (ALAS, rate-limiting), (2) GLUTATHIONE: Î³-Glu-Cys-GLY (antioxidant tripeptide), (3) PURINES: C4, C5, N7 of purine ring, (4) CREATINE: glycine + arginine â†’ guanidinoacetate â†’ creatine, (5) COLLAGEN: Gly-X-Y repeat (every 3rd position, ~33%), (6) CONJUGATION: glycine conjugation of bile acids (glycocholate) and drugs (hippuric acid from benzoate), (7) NEUROTRANSMITTER: inhibitory in spinal cord (strychnine = glycine receptor antagonist), (8) ONE-CARBON: glycine cleavage system â†’ N5,N10-methylene-THF. Non-ketotic hyperglycinemia: glycine cleavage system defect â†’ encephalopathy. Reference: Harper's â€” Glycine Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin is an example of which level of protein structure?
questions.optionB: Secondary
questions.optionC: Tertiary
questions.optionD: Primary
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin: quaternary structure â€” tetramer consisting of 2Î± and 2Î² globin subunits, each with a heme group (Fe2+ protoporphyrin IX). Exhibits cooperativity (sigmoid O2 dissociation curve) and allosteric regulation (2,3-DPG, CO2, H+, temperature). Myoglobin: single subunit â†’ tertiary structure only â†’ hyperbolic curve (no cooperativity). T-state (taut, deoxy): low affinity. R-state (relaxed, oxy): high affinity. Reference: Harper's â€” Hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: The isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid is the pH at which:
questions.optionA: The amino acid is fully protonated
questions.optionB: The amino acid precipitates
questions.optionC: The amino acid is fully deprotonated
questions.optionD: The amino acid has zero net charge (zwitterion form predominates)
questions.explanation: pI (isoelectric point): pH at which the amino acid or protein has zero net electrical charge and does not migrate in an electric field. For amino acids without ionizable side chains: pI = (pK1 + pK2)/2. For acidic amino acids: pI = (pK1 + pKR)/2. For basic amino acids: pI = (pK2 + pKR)/2. At pI, protein solubility is minimum â†’ casein precipitation (milk curdling). Electrophoresis separates proteins based on charge at given pH. Reference: Harper's â€” Isoelectric Point.

questions.questionText: HbS (Glu6Val Î²-chain) confers malaria resistance in heterozygotes (sickle cell trait). The biochemical mechanism of this protection is:
questions.optionA: Parasitized RBCs with HbAS sickle at LOW O2 â†’ sickling triggers premature splenic removal of infected cells AND reduces intraerythrocytic parasite growth due to HbS polymerization disrupting parasite feeding vacuole
questions.optionB: HbS kills the malaria parasite directly
questions.optionC: HbS blocks merozoite invasion
questions.optionD: HbS increases immune response to malaria
questions.explanation: Sickle trait and malaria resistance (balanced polymorphism): HbAS heterozygotes (~40% HbS): (1) Normal O2: no sickling â†’ no symptoms. (2) P. falciparum infection: parasite metabolism consumes O2 â†’ LOCAL HYPOXIA within infected RBC â†’ HbS polymerizes â†’ sickling. Consequences: (a) Sickling marks cell for SPLENIC REMOVAL (before parasite matures to schizont stage), (b) HbS polymers DISRUPT parasite feeding vacuole â†’ impaired hemoglobin digestion, (c) Increased oxidative stress in parasitized cells â†’ parasite death, (d) Reduced cytoadherence (sickled cells express less PfEMP1 â†’ less sequestration). This explains HbS gene frequency in malaria-endemic regions (Africa, Mediterranean, Middle East, India). G6PD deficiency, thalassemia also confer partial malaria resistance. Reference: Harper's â€” Sickle Trait Malaria.

questions.questionText: Disulfide bonds (Cys-S-S-Cys) stabilize protein tertiary/quaternary structure. Protein disulfide isomerase (PDI) in the ER ensures:
questions.optionB: Proteins never form disulfide bonds
questions.optionC: CORRECT disulfide bond pairing â€” PDI catalyzes breakage and reformation of incorrect disulfide bonds until the thermodynamically most stable (native) pairing is achieved; it is essential for proper folding of secretory and membrane proteins
questions.optionD: Disulfide bonds form in the cytoplasm
questions.explanation: Protein disulfide isomerase (PDI): ER lumen is OXIDIZING (unlike reducing cytoplasm) â†’ permits disulfide bond formation. Problem: random disulfide pairing among multiple Cys residues â†’ many incorrect combinations. PDI: thioredoxin-family enzyme with CXXC motif â†’ catalyzes: (1) OXIDATION: forms disulfide bonds (Cys-SH + SH-Cys â†’ Cys-S-S-Cys), (2) REDUCTION: breaks incorrect bonds, (3) ISOMERIZATION: rearranges incorrect to correct pairings. PDI is recycled by Ero1 (ER oxidoreductin 1, uses O2). Example: insulin: 3 disulfide bonds (2 interchain, 1 intrachain) â€” correct pairing essential. Immunoglobulins: multiple disulfide bonds. PDI deficiency â†’ protein misfolding â†’ ER stress â†’ unfolded protein response (UPR). Reference: Harper's â€” Disulfide Bond Formation.
questions.conceptTag: PDI disulfide isomerase ER protein folding

questions.questionText: Bradford and BCA assays are used to quantify total protein concentration. The principle underlying the Bradford assay is:
questions.optionB: UV absorption at 280nm only
questions.optionC: Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 dye binds to hydrophobic regions and positively charged residues (Arg, Lys, His) of proteins â†’ shifts dye absorption from 465nm (red/brown) to 595nm (blue) proportional to protein concentration
questions.optionD: Radioactive labeling of proteins
questions.explanation: Protein quantification methods: BRADFORD ASSAY: Coomassie blue G-250 binds proteins (hydrophobic + electrostatic interactions with Arg, Lys, His). Free dye: red/brown (Î»max 465nm). Bound dye: BLUE (Î»max 595nm). Advantages: fast (2 min), sensitive (1-20 Î¼g). Disadvantages: variable response to different proteins (detergent interference). BCA ASSAY (bicinchoninic acid): proteins reduce CuÂ²âº â†’ Cuâº (in alkaline, similar to biuret) â†’ Cuâº chelated by BCA â†’ purple complex (562nm). More uniform protein-to-protein response. LOWRY: Folin-Ciocalteu reagent + biuret reaction â†’ blue (750nm). Sensitive but many interferences. UV 280nm: Trp, Tyr, Phe absorption â€” no reagents but less sensitive. BIURET: simplest, least sensitive â†’ CuÂ²âº + peptide bonds (â‰¥2) â†’ violet complex (540nm). Reference: Harper's â€” Protein Assays.

questions.questionText: Proline is unique among amino acids because it:
questions.optionB: Has no amino group
questions.optionC: Is negatively charged
questions.optionD: Contains an imino group (pyrrolidine ring) â†’ introduces kinks/bends in protein chains
questions.explanation: Proline: imino acid (secondary amine â€” Î±-amino group is part of a rigid pyrrolidine ring). This restricts rotation of the polypeptide backbone â†’ introduces kinks/bends, disrupts Î±-helices and Î²-sheets. Proline is an 'Î±-helix breaker.' Important in collagen (Gly-X-Y, where X is often proline, Y is often hydroxyproline). Trans-cis isomerization of proline peptide bonds is catalyzed by prolyl isomerases (cyclophilin â€” target of cyclosporine). Reference: Harper's â€” Proline.

questions.questionText: Denaturation of proteins involves loss of:
questions.optionB: Only quaternary structure
questions.optionC: All levels of structure
questions.optionD: Secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure (but primary structure is preserved)
questions.explanation: Denaturation: disruption of non-covalent interactions (H-bonds, hydrophobic, ionic, van der Waals) and disulfide bonds â†’ loss of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure â†’ loss of biological activity. Primary structure (peptide bonds) is NOT broken. Caused by: heat, extreme pH, urea, guanidine HCl, organic solvents, heavy metals, detergents. Example: egg white coagulation (heat â†’ albumin denaturation). Renaturation possible in some cases (Anfinsen experiment â€” RNase A). Reference: Harper's â€” Denaturation.

questions.questionText: The amino acid that absorbs UV light at 280 nm (used for protein quantification) is primarily:
questions.optionB: Alanine
questions.optionC: Tryptophan (and tyrosine to a lesser extent)
questions.optionD: Leucine
questions.explanation: Aromatic amino acids absorb UV: Tryptophan (~280 nm, strongest absorber), Tyrosine (~275 nm), Phenylalanine (~258 nm). Protein concentration can be estimated by measuring absorbance at 280 nm (mainly due to Trp and Tyr). All amino acids absorb at 205-210 nm (peptide bond). DNA/RNA absorb at 260 nm (purine/pyrimidine bases). A260/A280 ratio used to assess protein purity. Reference: Harper's â€” UV Absorption.

questions.questionText: Glutathione is a tripeptide composed of:
questions.optionB: Î³-Glutamyl-Cysteinyl-Glycine
questions.optionC: Ala-Gly-Ser
questions.optionD: Phe-Gly-Trp
questions.explanation: Glutathione (GSH): Î³-glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine. The Î³-peptide bond (between Î³-COOH of glutamate and Î±-NH2 of cysteine) protects it from peptidase cleavage. Active -SH group of cysteine â†’ antioxidant. GSH + H2O2 â†’ GSSG (oxidized, via glutathione peroxidase, requires Se). GSSG â†’ GSH (glutathione reductase, requires NADPH from HMP shunt). G6PD deficiency â†’ low NADPH â†’ low GSH â†’ hemolytic anemia under oxidative stress. Reference: Harper's â€” Glutathione.

questions.questionText: Chaperones (heat shock proteins) assist in:
questions.optionB: Proper protein folding and preventing misfolding/aggregation
questions.optionC: Lipid synthesis
questions.optionD: Carbohydrate digestion
questions.explanation: Molecular chaperones (HSP70, HSP60/chaperonins, HSP90): assist newly synthesized polypeptides in achieving correct 3D folding, prevent aggregation under stress (heat, oxidative). They do NOT provide structural information â€” the folding code is in the amino acid sequence (Anfinsen's dogma). Chaperonin (GroEL/GroES in bacteria, HSP60/10 in eukaryotes): provides isolated chamber for protein folding. Misfolded proteins â†’ proteasome (ubiquitin-tagged) or cause diseases (Alzheimer's, prion). Reference: Harper's â€” Chaperones.

questions.questionText: Albumin (68 kDa, pI 4.7) is the most abundant plasma protein. In nephrotic syndrome, the specific reason albumin is preferentially lost in urine is:
questions.optionB: Albumin production is increased
questions.optionC: Albumin is actively secreted into urine
questions.optionD: The glomerular filtration barrier has both SIZE and CHARGE selectivity â€” albumin's relatively small size (68 kDa, near filtration cutoff) combined with LOSS OF NEGATIVE CHARGE on the GBM (heparan sulfate proteoglycan depletion) in nephrotic syndrome allows albumin passage
questions.explanation: Albumin and glomerular filtration: Normal glomerular barrier: (1) SIZE barrier: podocyte slit diaphragm (~40nm pores) â†’ excludes proteins >60 kDa, (2) CHARGE barrier: GBM rich in HEPARAN SULFATE PROTEOGLYCANS â†’ negative charge â†’ repels negatively charged proteins. Albumin: 68 kDa (borderline for size barrier) + pI 4.7 (NEGATIVELY charged at physiological pH) â†’ DOUBLY protected. In nephrotic syndrome (minimal change, FSGS, membranous): loss of podocyte foot processes + depletion of GBM negative charges â†’ both barriers compromised â†’ massive albuminuria (>3.5g/day). Consequences: hypoalbuminemia â†’ decreased oncotic pressure â†’ EDEMA. Compensatory hepatic protein synthesis â†’ hyperlipidemia (increased lipoprotein synthesis). Loss of anticoagulant proteins (AT-III) â†’ hypercoagulability. Reference: Harper's â€” Albumin Nephrotic Syndrome.
questions.conceptTag: Albumin GBM charge size nephrotic syndrome

questions.questionText: The amino acid that is both glucogenic and ketogenic is:
questions.optionB: Glycine
questions.optionC: Isoleucine (and also tryptophan, phenylalanine, tyrosine, threonine)
questions.optionD: Alanine
questions.explanation: Classification: Purely ketogenic: leucine, lysine (only produce acetyl-CoA/acetoacetyl-CoA). Both glucogenic and ketogenic: isoleucine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, tyrosine, threonine (produce both glucogenic and ketogenic intermediates). All other amino acids are purely glucogenic (produce pyruvate, oxaloacetate, Î±-KG, succinyl-CoA, fumarate). Mnemonic for purely ketogenic: 'LL' (Leucine, Lysine). Reference: Harper's â€” Amino Acid Catabolism.

questions.questionText: HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) is formed by:
questions.optionB: Acetylation of Hb
questions.optionC: Phosphorylation of Hb
questions.optionD: Non-enzymatic glycation of Hb with glucose (Amadori rearrangement)
questions.explanation: HbA1c: formed by non-enzymatic glycation (Maillard reaction) â€” glucose reacts with N-terminal valine of the Î²-chain via Schiff base â†’ Amadori rearrangement â†’ stable ketoamine (HbA1c). Reflects average blood glucose over preceding 2-3 months (RBC lifespan ~120 days). Normal: <5.7%. Prediabetes: 5.7-6.4%. Diabetes: â‰¥6.5%. Used for diagnosis and monitoring of diabetes. Falsely elevated: iron deficiency. Falsely low: hemolysis, transfusion. Reference: Harper's â€” HbA1c.

questions.questionText: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is most commonly caused by defects in:
questions.optionB: Fibronectin
questions.optionC: Keratin
questions.optionD: Collagen (various types â€” synthesis, structure, or processing defects)
questions.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: group of connective tissue disorders due to defects in collagen synthesis, structure, or processing. Features: hyperextensible skin, joint hypermobility, tissue fragility, easy bruising. Multiple types: Classic (COL5A), Vascular (COL3A1 â€” most severe, arterial rupture), Hypermobility, Kyphoscoliotic (lysyl hydroxylase deficiency). Marfan syndrome: fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defect. Osteogenesis imperfecta: Type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2). Reference: Harper's â€” Collagen Disorders.

questions.questionText: The Edman degradation is used to determine:
questions.optionB: Amino acid sequence from the N-terminal end (one residue at a time)
questions.optionC: 3D structure of proteins
questions.optionD: Disulfide bond positions
questions.explanation: Edman degradation: sequential removal and identification of amino acids from the N-terminal end using phenylisothiocyanate (PITC/Edman reagent). PITC reacts with free Î±-amino group â†’ thiazolinone derivative â†’ cleaved â†’ identified by chromatography â†’ repeat. Can sequence ~50-60 residues from N-terminal. Longer proteins: fragmented first (trypsin, cyanogen bromide), then fragments sequenced. Now largely replaced by mass spectrometry. Reference: Harper's â€” Protein Sequencing.

questions.questionText: Nitrogen balance studies reveal that a patient in negative nitrogen balance could be in any of the following states EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Active growth phase in children (positive balance â€” N intake > N excretion due to net protein synthesis for growth)
questions.optionC: Severe burns
questions.optionD: Cancer cachexia
questions.explanation: Nitrogen balance â€” clinical states: N balance = N intake (dietary protein Ã· 6.25) - N output (urinary urea N + fecal N + skin/sweat N). POSITIVE: N intake > output (net protein synthesis): growing children, pregnancy, recovery from illness, anabolic steroids, refeeding. NEGATIVE: N output > intake (net protein catabolism): starvation, sepsis, major surgery, burns, trauma, cancer cachexia, immobilization, corticosteroid therapy, uncontrolled diabetes. EQUILIBRIUM: healthy adults on adequate diet (N in = N out). Clinical uses: assess protein adequacy in ICU (target positive balance for recovery). 1g urinary N â‰ˆ 6.25g protein catabolized. 24-hour urine urea nitrogen (UUN) + 4g (insensible losses) = total N output. Reference: Harper's â€” Nitrogen Balance.
questions.conceptTag: Nitrogen balance positive negative clinical states

questions.questionText: A young man with emphysema at an early age is suspected to have alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency. This primarily reflects a defect in which protein class and function?
questions.optionA: Plasma serine protease inhibitor protecting elastin from neutrophil elastase
questions.optionB: Structural collagen involved in tensile strength
questions.optionC: Contractile actin in alveolar smooth muscle
questions.optionD: Membrane transporter in chloride channel family
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Alpha1-antitrypsin is a serpin (serine protease inhibitor) synthesized in liver; it inhibits neutrophil elastase and protects elastin in lung parenchyma. Deficiency causes early panacinar emphysema and liver disease due to misfolded protein accumulation. A and C are unrelated primary mechanisms; D describes CFTR pathology.

questions.questionText: Which feature best distinguishes myoglobin from hemoglobin?
questions.optionA: Myoglobin has quaternary structure with cooperative oxygen binding
questions.optionB: Myoglobin is a monomer with hyperbolic oxygen dissociation curve
questions.optionC: Myoglobin binds 2,3-BPG strongly and shifts curve to right
questions.optionD: Myoglobin carries most oxygen in blood plasma
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Myoglobin is monomeric (single polypeptide + heme) with high oxygen affinity and hyperbolic binding curve. Hemoglobin is tetrameric, cooperative, and allosterically regulated by 2,3-BPG, H+, and CO2. Myoglobin primarily stores oxygen in muscle.

questions.questionText: Which amino acid is most likely to be enriched in keratin and contributes to hardness of nails/hair via cross-linking?
questions.optionB: Glycine
questions.optionC: Tyrosine
questions.optionD: Cysteine
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Keratin is rich in cysteine; extensive disulfide cross-links (cystine) increase mechanical strength and hardness, especially in hair and nails. Glycine is more characteristic of collagen repeat abundance.

questions.questionText: Which is the MOST appropriate statement regarding elastin?
questions.optionA: It has Gly-X-Y triplet as dominant sequence like collagen
questions.optionB: It provides elasticity and uses lysyl oxidase-dependent cross-links
questions.optionC: Its hydroxylation of proline is the key determinant of recoil
questions.optionD: It is the main oxygen-carrying protein in muscle
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Elastin provides stretch-recoil properties in vessels, lungs, and skin; cross-linking involves lysyl oxidase (copper-dependent), forming desmosine/isodesmosine links. A and C are collagen-centric statements; D is myoglobin function.

questions.questionText: In serum protein electrophoresis at alkaline pH, albumin migrates fastest toward anode mainly because it:
questions.optionB: Has highest positive charge
questions.optionC: Has highest net negative charge
questions.optionD: Is glycosylated more than all globulins
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. At alkaline pH, albumin is strongly negatively charged and migrates fastest to anode. It does not have highest molecular weight; migration depends on charge-to-mass characteristics and buffer pH.

questions.questionText: A patient with poor wound healing and bleeding gums has normal dietary protein intake but vitamin C deficiency. The immediate biochemical defect is impaired:
questions.optionB: Hydroxylation of proline/lysine residues in procollagen
questions.optionC: Disulfide bond formation in keratin
questions.optionD: Transamination of branched-chain amino acids
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Vitamin C is required for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase reactions during collagen maturation in ER. Defective hydroxylation reduces collagen stability, causing scurvy features.

questions.questionText: Which statement about denaturation is CORRECT?
questions.optionA: It always breaks peptide bonds first
questions.optionB: It can abolish enzymatic activity without altering primary sequence
questions.optionC: It invariably causes complete irreversible hydrolysis
questions.optionD: It increases specificity of enzymes
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Denaturation disrupts higher-order folding (secondary/tertiary/quaternary), commonly abolishing biological function while primary peptide sequence remains intact. Peptide bond hydrolysis requires stronger chemical/enzymatic conditions.

questions.questionText: The buffering property of histidine in proteins is mainly due to which side-chain group?
questions.optionB: Guanidinium group
questions.optionC: Imidazole ring
questions.optionD: Thioether sulfur
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Histidine contains an imidazole ring with pKa near physiological range, making it an effective proton donor/acceptor and important in enzyme active sites and hemoglobin buffering.

questions.questionText: Ninhydrin reacts with amino acids to produce a purple/blue color (Ruhemann's purple). The exception is proline, which gives:
questions.optionA: A YELLOW color â€” because proline is an IMINO acid (secondary amine, Î±-amino group is part of pyrrolidine ring), which reacts differently with ninhydrin to produce a yellow derivative instead of Ruhemann's purple
questions.optionB: No color
questions.optionC: Green color
questions.optionD: Red color
questions.explanation: Ninhydrin reaction â€” biochemical basis: Ninhydrin (triketohydrindene hydrate): reacts with Î±-amino acids in a multi-step reaction: (1) Oxidative deamination of AA â†’ aldehyde + NH3 + CO2, (2) Released NH3 reacts with 2 molecules of ninhydrin â†’ RUHEMANN'S PURPLE (diketohydrindylidene-diketohydrindamine, Î»max 570nm). PROLINE exception: secondary amine (N is part of 5-membered pyrrolidine ring) â†’ cannot undergo standard ninhydrin reaction â†’ instead forms a YELLOW product (Î»max 440nm). Hydroxyproline: same yellow reaction. Clinical/lab uses: (1) Amino acid detection in chromatography (TLC, paper), (2) Quantification of amino acids (amino acid analyzer), (3) Forensic: fingerprint detection (latent prints contain AA from sweat â†’ ninhydrin spray â†’ purple fingerprints). (4) Urinary amino acid screening (aminoaciduria). Reference: Harper's â€” Ninhydrin.

questions.questionText: A mutation replacing glycine in collagen triple helix is particularly disruptive because glycine:
questions.optionA: Is acidic and forms ionic bonds
questions.optionB: Is aromatic and stabilizes pi-stacking
questions.optionC: Contains sulfur needed for cross-linking
questions.optionD: Is the only residue small enough to fit in helix core every third position
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Collagen requires glycine at every third residue for tight packing of triple helix core. Substitution disrupts helix and can cause disorders such as osteogenesis imperfecta (with COL1 defects).

questions.questionText: The most appropriate interpretation of low plasma albumin with pedal edema in chronic liver disease is:
questions.optionA: Reduced hepatic albumin synthesis causing decreased oncotic pressure
questions.optionB: Increased plasma oncotic pressure
questions.optionC: Increased globulin synthesis causing edema
questions.optionD: Primary renal glucose wasting
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Liver synthesizes albumin; chronic liver dysfunction lowers albumin, decreases plasma colloid osmotic pressure, and promotes fluid shift into interstitium causing edema/ascites.

questions.questionText: Which pair is correctly matched regarding structural proteins?
questions.optionB: Elastin - predominant Gly-X-Y repeat with triple helix
questions.optionC: Collagen - rich in hydroxyproline and tensile strength
questions.optionD: Myosin - major dermal fiber protein
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Collagen is rich in glycine/proline/hydroxyproline and provides tensile strength. Elastin provides recoil and does not have classical collagen triple-helix architecture. Keratin is an intracellular/intermediate filament protein, not proteoglycan.

questions.questionText: Prion diseases (transmissible spongiform encephalopathies) are caused by:
questions.optionA: Conformational change of normal PrPC protein to misfolded PrPSc (Î²-sheet rich, protease-resistant form)
questions.optionB: A virus with RNA genome
questions.optionC: Bacterial toxin
questions.optionD: Autoimmune antibodies against neurons
questions.explanation: Prion diseases: infectious protein (no nucleic acid). Normal PrPC (predominantly Î±-helical, protease-sensitive) â†’ misfolds to PrPSc (predominantly Î²-sheet, protease-resistant). PrPSc acts as a template â†’ converts more PrPC to PrPSc â†’ aggregation â†’ spongiform degeneration of brain. Examples: CJD (Creutzfeldt-Jakob), kuru, BSE (mad cow), fatal familial insomnia, Gerstmann-StrÃ¤ussler-Scheinker. Diagnosis: 14-3-3 protein in CSF, RT-QuIC assay. No treatment. Reference: Harper's â€” Prion Protein Misfolding.

questions.questionText: In amyloidosis, the characteristic staining pattern is:
questions.optionB: Congo red positive with apple-green birefringence under polarized light
questions.optionC: Gram positive
questions.optionD: Silver stain positive
questions.explanation: Amyloid: misfolded proteins deposited extracellularly as insoluble fibrils rich in Î²-pleated sheet structure. Staining: Congo red â†’ salmon pink under light microscopy â†’ APPLE-GREEN BIREFRINGENCE under polarized light (pathognomonic). Types: AL amyloid (immunoglobulin light chains â€” multiple myeloma), AA amyloid (serum amyloid A â€” chronic inflammation), AÎ² (Alzheimer's), ATTR (transthyretin â€” familial/senile). Organs: kidney (nephrotic syndrome), heart, liver, tongue. Reference: Harper's â€” Amyloid Structure.

questions.questionText: Type IV collagen is found exclusively in basement membranes. Unlike fibrillar collagens (I, II, III), type IV collagen:
questions.optionB: Is not a collagen at all
questions.optionC: Does NOT form fibrils; instead it forms a mesh-like NETWORK (sheet/lattice) because it retains its globular NC1 domains and contains interruptions in the Gly-X-Y repeat, enabling lateral associations that create the scaffold of basement membranes
questions.optionD: Contains no glycine
questions.explanation: Collagen types â€” fibrillar vs network: FIBRILLAR (I, II, III, V, XI): processed into tropocollagen â†’ assemble into quarter-staggered fibrils â†’ fibers (providing tensile strength). Type I: bone, tendon, skin (most abundant). Type II: cartilage, vitreous. Type III: blood vessels, skin (reticular). NETWORK (Type IV): (1) NOT processed â€” retains NC1 C-terminal and 7S N-terminal globular domains, (2) Has ~22 interruptions in Gly-X-Y repeat â†’ flexibility, (3) NC1 domains of different molecules interact â†’ head-to-head dimers, (4) 7S domains interact â†’ tetramers, (5) Lateral associations â†’ chicken-wire network â†’ scaffold of ALL basement membranes. Goodpasture syndrome: autoantibodies against NC1 domain of Î±3 chain of type IV collagen (Î±3(IV)NC1) â†’ GBM and alveolar BM damage â†’ glomerulonephritis + pulmonary hemorrhage. Alport syndrome: mutations in Î±3/Î±4/Î±5(IV) chains. Reference: Harper's â€” Type IV Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: Type IV collagen network basement membrane Goodpasture

questions.questionText: SDS-PAGE separates proteins based on:
questions.optionA: Molecular weight (SDS denatures and coats all proteins with uniform negative charge)
questions.optionB: Charge and isoelectric point
questions.optionC: Biological activity
questions.optionD: Hydrophobicity only
questions.explanation: SDS-PAGE: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) denatures proteins and coats them with uniform negative charge proportional to length â†’ migration depends solely on molecular weight (smaller proteins migrate faster). Î²-mercaptoethanol or DTT reduces disulfide bonds. Unlike native PAGE (which separates by charge and size), SDS-PAGE separates by size only. Western blot: SDS-PAGE followed by transfer to membrane â†’ probed with specific antibody â†’ detects specific proteins. 2D gel: first dimension = IEF (charge), second = SDS-PAGE (size). Reference: Harper's â€” Protein Electrophoresis.

questions.questionText: Osteogenesis imperfecta is most commonly caused by mutations in genes encoding:
questions.optionB: Elastin
questions.optionC: Type I collagen (COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes)
questions.optionD: Fibrillin
questions.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease): defective Type I collagen (quantitative or qualitative defect in COL1A1/COL1A2). Features: fragile bones with recurrent fractures, blue sclerae (thin sclera â†’ choroidal veins visible), hearing loss (otosclerosis), dentinogenesis imperfecta. Type I: mild, AD, reduced amount of normal collagen (null allele). Type II: lethal perinatal, AD (new mutation). Type III: severe, progressive deformity. Type IV: moderate. Marfan syndrome: fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defect. Reference: Harper's â€” OI.

questions.questionText: At the isoelectric point (pI), a protein has specific properties that are exploited in separation techniques. At pI:
questions.optionB: The protein denatures
questions.optionC: The protein has zero net charge â†’ MINIMUM solubility (maximum precipitation), no electrophoretic migration, and maximum crystallization tendency â€” this is why isoelectric precipitation is used for casein isolation (pI 4.6) and immunoglobulin purification
questions.optionD: The protein is maximally active
questions.explanation: Isoelectric point â€” practical applications: At pI: net charge = 0 â†’ no charge-charge repulsion between protein molecules â†’ MINIMUM SOLUBILITY (maximum protein-protein interaction â†’ aggregation/precipitation). Applications: (1) Isoelectric precipitation: add acid to milk â†’ pH reaches casein pI (4.6) â†’ casein precipitates (cheese making). (2) Salting out at pI: immunoglobulin purification â€” IgG pI ~7.5, adjust pH + ammonium sulfate â†’ selective precipitation. (3) IEF (isoelectric focusing): separate proteins by pI in pH gradient. (4) 2D-PAGE: first dimension IEF (pI), second dimension SDS-PAGE (MW). pI calculation (simple amino acid): pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2 (for neutral AA). For acidic AA: average two lowest pKas. For basic AA: average two highest pKas. Reference: Harper's â€” Isoelectric Point Applications.

questions.questionText: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vascular type IV) is caused by defective:
questions.optionB: Type I collagen
questions.optionC: Elastin
questions.optionD: Fibronectin
questions.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS): group of connective tissue disorders. Vascular type (Type IV): COL3A1 mutation â†’ defective Type III collagen â†’ most dangerous â€” arterial/intestinal/uterine rupture, easy bruising, thin translucent skin. Classical type (I/II): COL5A mutation â†’ joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, poor wound healing (cigarette-paper scars). Kyphoscoliotic type: lysyl hydroxylase deficiency â†’ similar + kyphoscoliosis, ocular fragility. Molecular type (VII): defective procollagen peptidase â†’ severe joint hypermobility. Reference: Harper's â€” EDS Types.

questions.questionText: Scurvy (vitamin C deficiency) causes collagen dysfunction. The specific hydroxylation reaction that fails is:
questions.optionB: Tyrosine hydroxylation
questions.optionC: Prolyl 4-hydroxylase (FeÂ²âº, Oâ‚‚, ascorbate, Î±-KG) fails to convert proline â†’ 4-hydroxyproline; unhydroxylated collagen cannot form stable triple helix at body temperature because hydroxyproline is critical for interchain hydrogen bonds via its OH group bridging to water molecules
questions.optionD: Tryptophan hydroxylation
questions.explanation: Vitamin C â†’ collagen hydroxylation â†’ scurvy: Prolyl 4-hydroxylase: FeÂ²âº/Î±-ketoglutarate dioxygenase. Reaction: proline + Oâ‚‚ + Î±-KG â†’ 4-hydroxyproline + COâ‚‚ + succinate. FeÂ²âº cycles: FeÂ²âº â†’ FeÂ³âº â†’ FeÂ²âº (ascorbate REDUCES FeÂ³âº back to FeÂ²âº â€” keeps enzyme active). Without ascorbate: FeÂ²âº â†’ FeÂ³âº (inactive, cannot be recycled). Role of Hyp: 4-hydroxyproline-OH forms INTERCHAIN hydrogen bonds via WATER BRIDGES (Hyp-OH---Hâ‚‚O---C=O of adjacent chain) â†’ stabilizes triple helix. Hyp content determines Tm (melting temperature) of collagen: normal collagen Tm ~42Â°C (above body temp 37Â°C â†’ stable). Unhydroxylated collagen Tm ~24Â°C â†’ UNSTABLE at 37Â°C â†’ denatures â†’ weak collagen. Scurvy manifestations: bleeding gums, petechiae, poor wound healing, corkscrew hairs, subperiosteal hemorrhage, tooth loss. Reference: Harper's â€” Vitamin C Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: Prolyl hydroxylase ascorbate hydroxyproline triple helix

questions.questionText: Marfan syndrome is caused by a mutation in:
questions.optionB: Elastin
questions.optionC: Fibrillin-1 (FBN1 gene, chromosome 15)
questions.optionD: Type IV collagen
questions.explanation: Marfan syndrome: autosomal dominant, FBN1 gene mutation â†’ defective fibrillin-1 â†’ abnormal microfibrils (scaffolding for elastic fibers). Also leads to increased TGF-Î² signaling. Features: tall stature, arachnodactyly (long spider-like fingers), pectus deformity, ectopia lentis (upward lens subluxation â€” unlike Homocystinuria: downward), mitral valve prolapse, aortic root dilation â†’ dissection (main cause of death). Abraham Lincoln suspected to have had Marfan. Reference: Harper's â€” Fibrillin and Marfan.

questions.questionText: Western blot technique is used for detection of:
questions.optionB: Lipids
questions.optionC: RNA
questions.optionD: Specific proteins (separated by SDS-PAGE, transferred to membrane, detected by antibody)
questions.explanation: Blotting techniques: Southern blot: DNA detection (named after Edwin Southern). Northern blot: RNA detection (named by analogy). Western blot: protein detection â€” proteins separated by SDS-PAGE â†’ transferred to nitrocellulose/PVDF membrane â†’ probed with primary antibody (specific) â†’ secondary antibody (enzyme-conjugated) â†’ detection. Used clinically as confirmatory test for HIV (detects anti-HIV antibodies against gp41, gp120, p24, etc.). ELISA is screening test for HIV. Reference: Harper's â€” Blotting Techniques.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin displays cooperative oxygen binding (sigmoidal curve). The molecular mechanism underlying cooperativity is:
questions.optionA: T-state to R-state conformational transition: Oâ‚‚ binding to one subunit induces conformational changes (breaking intersubunit salt bridges) transmitted to other subunits via the Î±1Î²2 interface â†’ progressively increasing Oâ‚‚ affinity (Hill coefficient ~2.8)
questions.optionB: Independent Oâ‚‚ binding to each subunit
questions.optionC: Oâ‚‚ binding is all-or-none
questions.optionD: Each subunit has different Oâ‚‚ affinity that doesn't change
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin cooperativity â€” molecular mechanism: T-state (tense, deoxy): low Oâ‚‚ affinity. Stabilized by: (1) Salt bridges between subunits (His146-Asp94, Lys40-His146), (2) 2,3-BPG binding in central cavity (Î²-subunit contacts), (3) His F8-Fe-heme geometry: FeÂ²âº slightly out of porphyrin plane. Oâ‚‚ binds first subunit â†’ FeÂ²âº pulled INTO porphyrin plane â†’ His F8 moves â†’ conformational change transmitted across Î±1Î²2 SLIDING INTERFACE â†’ salt bridges break progressively â†’ Tâ†’R transition. R-state (relaxed, oxy): HIGH Oâ‚‚ affinity. Central cavity narrowed â†’ 2,3-BPG expelled. Cooperativity: binding of each Oâ‚‚ increases affinity for next â†’ SIGMOIDAL curve (Hill equation: n = 2.8). Myoglobin: single subunit â†’ HYPERBOLIC curve (n = 1, no cooperativity). Bohr effect: Hâº/COâ‚‚ stabilize T-state â†’ right-shift â†’ Oâ‚‚ unloading at tissues. Reference: Harper's â€” Cooperativity.
